reasoning: The analysis is performed on the provided incomplete disassembly containing several functional sections (.init, .plt, .plt.sec, and parts of .text). Due to the missing sections, large gaps in the function bodies, and hidden control flow paths in non-retrieved fragments, the full control flow graph cannot be determined.
A partial CFG is reconstructed from distinct well-formed subroutines present in the available .text snippet.

In the entry/init function (.text base 0x24c0), control starts at B0, sets up parameters, invokes an external function through a PLT slot via a call at 0x24df, and immediately reaches a terminal halt instruction (hlt) at B1.

At address 0x24f0, a separate function snippet evaluates a conditional branch based on string/pointer comparison (cmp/je). If true, it jumps to a exit/return path at 0x2518 (B4); if false, it performs an indirect jump via a pointer loaded into rax (B3).

At address 0x2520, another standard block sequence calculates an index, applies sign extension/shifts, and conditionally branches via 'je 2558'. If not taken, it tests a global/GOT pointer and performs an indirect jmp rax, otherwise reaching a terminal ret instruction.

At address 0x2560, a clear conditional structure (cmp / jne) sets up a typical short-circuit evaluation or initialization check. If the byte flag is non-zero, it skips execution directly to ret at 0x2598 (B11). Otherwise, it proceeds to check another pointer alignment, optionally invoking a finalizer routine before returning.
Further small functions (such as at 0x25a9 and 0x25e5) demonstrate sequential stack setup, calls to external library wrappers (tcsetattr), and clean leaf-node function epilogues (leave/ret).
Because these components represent separate disconnected sections of a larger binary logic, the nodes are numbered sequentially according to their local control dependencies.
